title: Personal study plan: capacity, learning functions, and recovery

objectives.statement: Construct a weekly capacity boundary that records fixed commitments, usable study blocks, an equivalent access route, and protected recovery capacity.
Allocate concept learning, problems, supervised lab work or lab preparation and debrief, delayed review, and recovery in a dependency-aware weekly sequence.
Revise a later week from completion and error-log evidence without converting every missed task into a growing backlog.

scenes.title: Plan for a week that can change
scenes.contentHtml:
Plan for a week that can change

A study plan is useful only when it can answer a concrete question: what will you do, in what order, with the capacity you actually have, and what will change when evidence or access changes? A list of intentions cannot answer that question by itself.

In this lesson, a sustainable plan means a plan that is internally feasible, includes the learning functions required by the course, protects some recovery capacity, and has a rule for revision. “Sustainable” is not a judgment about effort or character, and this lesson does not prescribe a universal number of hours.

You will build a week that makes five functions visible:

  1. concept learning;
  2. problem solving;
  3. institutionally supervised lab work, or lab preparation and debrief when a lab is scheduled;
  4. retrieval and review, including a later contact with earlier learning; and
  5. recovery capacity or a reduced re-entry route.

The week will also use evidence from the prerequisite error log. If an error identifies an insecure prerequisite, the plan protects a targeted repair before dependent work. If evidence shows a task is secure or optional, the plan can reduce or remove redundant work.

The four-pass model used here is:

  1. bound capacity;
  2. place dependencies and fixed work;
  3. distribute learning functions; and
  4. reserve and revise.

This is an instructional model for the assigned curriculum outcome, not a uniquely optimal productivity system.

Scope and exclusions

This lesson teaches schedule reasoning. It does not diagnose fatigue, disability, illness, or difficulty; provide health or sleep advice; guarantee grades or admission; override institutional deadlines; or authorize unsupervised laboratory activity. Use generic labels such as “fixed commitment” or “low-access block” instead of recording private details.

Recovery capacity (term-recovery-capacity) is capacity deliberately left uncommitted or assigned to a reduced route. It is part of the plan, not a reward earned after every other box is checked.

Source Note
claims claim-plan-balance, claim-recovery-route, claim-four-pass-modelsources source-premed-graph, source-premed-syllabus, source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence

The target and five required functions come from the graph and course contract. The four passes are this lesson's original synthesis of those requirements with the repository's bounded evidence on retrieval, spacing, guidance, and recovery; no retained source establishes the procedure as universally optimal.

scenes.title: Retrieve the evidence-to-action chain
scenes.contentHtml:
Retrieve the evidence-to-action chain

Do this check before reading the feedback. It is low stakes and does not ask for private schedule details.

Check
id check-error-to-action

Choose one fictional or real-but-anonymized weak curriculum node. From memory, write:

  1. the node or a generic label for it;
  2. the error class shown by the latest attempt;
  3. the earliest insecure prerequisite, if one is implicated;
  4. one targeted next action; and
  5. the fresh evidence that would show whether the action worked.

Check after attempting

A complete chain might read:

Node: interpreting a line graph. Error: REP, because the verbal trend was accurate but the axes were reversed. Earliest insecure prerequisite: identifying what each axis measures. Next action: complete one contrasting graph example and explain each axis before interpreting the trend. Fresh evidence: interpret a different graph without prompts, then retrieve the axis rule later.

The important relation is not the particular error code. The next action follows from the observed error and its prerequisite. “Study harder” is too broad because it does not specify what representation, relation, or decision will change.

If you can produce that chain, carry its targeted action into the weekly plan. If you cannot, do not invent a priority from confidence or urgency alone.

Recovery route

Return to topic-orientation-metacognition-and-error-log. Classify one missed response, identify its earliest insecure prerequisite, and choose one contrasting repair task plus fresh evidence. Then repeat this diagnostic. Missing a prerequisite is routing evidence, not failure.

Source Note
claims claim-error-directed-revisionsources source-premed-syllabus

The course recovery protocol supports the node-to-error-to-prerequisite-to-repair chain used by this diagnostic. It does not establish the personal cause of a missed response.

scenes.title: Build the four-pass planning model
scenes.contentHtml:
Build the four-pass planning model

A weekly plan is a constraint-and-evidence model. It represents what is fixed, what can move, what each activity is meant to produce, and what happens if the first route becomes unavailable.

Pass 1: bound capacity

Start with a capacity boundary (term-capacity-boundary): the study opportunities that are realistically usable after fixed commitments and access constraints are represented. A study block (term-study-block) is a learner-defined planning unit, not a universal duration. One learner may use equal blocks; another may label them short, standard, and extended. The plan only requires that the labels are consistent enough to prevent double-booking.

Record four kinds of constraint:

Constraint Question Neutral record
Fixed commitment What cannot move inside this week? “Fixed commitment A”
Access When are equipment, supervision, connectivity, or a quiet text route available? “Online access available”
Usable study capacity Which opportunities can genuinely hold a learning task? “Ten usable blocks”
Recovery capacity Which capacity remains uncommitted or reduced? “Two reserve blocks”

Do not write medical, disability, employment, or caregiving details. The plan needs the constraint, not its private cause.

Pass 2: place dependencies and fixed work

A fixed commitment (term-fixed-commitment) is time- or access-bound. Place institutionally supervised labs and true deadlines first. Then annotate dependencies:

  • concept acquisition before a dependent problem or investigation;
  • lab preparation before the supervised session;
  • lab debrief after the session;
  • targeted prerequisite repair before another task that uses the failed relation; and
  • an earlier learning episode before its delayed retrieval contact.

Priority is therefore relational. A short prerequisite repair can outrank a larger assignment when the larger task depends on that prerequisite.

Pass 3: distribute learning functions

A learning function (term-learning-function) is the job an activity performs. “Study biology” hides the job; “retrieve the transport model from memory, then explain one failed prediction” exposes it.

The course distinguishes acquisition, retrieval, problem solving, investigation, application, and reflection/planning. One block may serve more than one function, but each promised function needs observable evidence. For example, reading a worked solution may support concept construction; it is not automatically evidence of independent problem solving.

Plan a delayed retrieval contact (term-delayed-retrieval-contact) after the initial learning episode. The useful interval depends on the retention goal, material, prior knowledge, and retrieval success. The model therefore requires a visible later contact but does not impose one fixed number of days.

Pass 4: reserve and revise

Protect recovery capacity before disruption occurs. Add a contingency (term-contingency) for a task whose medium or access could fail. A contingency preserves the learning operation where possible: an offline text explanation and typed graph interpretation can replace an online display, whereas “skip all reasoning” cannot.

At weekly review, classify each unfinished or ineffective task with a revision decision (term-revision-decision):

  • protect because it is a required outcome, prerequisite, fixed event, or due retrieval;
  • move because it remains necessary and another feasible slot exists;
  • split because a smaller coherent part can be completed without losing the target operation;
  • replace because another task better addresses the same evidence; or
  • remove because it is redundant, optional, or no longer serves the mapped outcome.

Do not copy every task forward. Use completion evidence, error classes, prerequisite relations, and due retrieval to decide.

Read the complete ledger

The planning representation is a text ledger:

Block Constraint or activity Learning function Why now Evidence Contingency
A Fixed commitment Lab Supervision available Recorded participation plus later debrief Institutional rescheduling route
B Example activity Concept Prerequisite for C Explained relation Offline text version
C Example activity Problem Applies B Independent solution and check Split into representation and execution
R Uncommitted Recovery Absorbs disruption Not counted as completed study Reduced route if used

Read row by row in chronological order. The table does not encode meaning by color or position alone.

Accessibility and alternatives

Use a calendar, paper, spreadsheet, or plain text. The equivalent text form for each entry is: “When or sequence; activity; function; dependency; evidence; contingency.” No task requires dragging, color, speech, precise pointer movement, continuous connectivity, or a timed response. If a lab is inaccessible, use the institution's approved accommodation or alternative; this lesson does not invent a substitute and does not claim physical skill from a non-physical route.

Source Note
claims claim-plan-balance, claim-course-cycle, claim-retrieval-spacing, claim-error-directed-revision, claim-recovery-route, claim-four-pass-modelsources source-premed-graph, source-premed-syllabus, source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence

The course sources support the required functions, error-directed recovery, and protected re-entry routes. The evidence synthesis supports retrieval, spacing, and explicit novice guidance with stated moderators. The four-pass ledger is an original bounded inference, not a measured optimal timetable.

scenes.title: Work a constrained week
scenes.contentHtml:
Work a constrained week

This fictional example models every decision. The numbers make the reasoning inspectable; they are not recommendations for block length or weekly hours.

Worked Example
id worked-example-twelve-block-week

Task. Build a week for Rowan with concept learning, problems, a supervised lab, review, and recovery. Explain the order and the next revision rule.

Givens. Rowan has twelve self-defined usable blocks. A supervised lab occupies two fixed consecutive blocks on Wednesday. A preparation task must precede it, and a debrief must follow it. The error log shows a REP representation-translation gap in a prerequisite measurement topic. A cumulative retrieval contact with an earlier node is due this week. No personal reason for the capacity limit is needed.

Constraints and unknowns. The fixed lab cannot move. The plan must not exceed twelve blocks. Unknowns are which flexible tasks to choose, where to place them, how much reserve to protect, and what evidence will drive revision.

Model selection. The four-pass model fits because the task combines a capacity limit, dependency order, distinct learning functions, and uncertainty about disruption.

Plan. First bound twelve blocks. Next anchor the lab and its preparation/debrief. Then protect the error-linked prerequisite repair and the due retrieval. Place concept work before dependent problems. Finally, leave two blocks as recovery capacity.

Execution.

Sequence Activity Function and reason Evidence or contingency
1 Contrast two measurement representations Concept and error repair; targets the REP prerequisite first Explain how the same quantity appears in words and a graph
2 Complete one near problem Problem solving after the repair Independent representation, execution, and check
3 Retrieve an earlier node before feedback Review; cumulative contact is due Written reconstruction, correction after attempt
4 Prepare the supervised lab Lab preparation before the fixed session State variables, measurement plan, and institutional safety route
5–6 Attend the fixed supervised lab Investigation under supervision Record the modality actually completed; follow institutional procedure
7 Debrief the lab evidence Lab debrief after the session Distinguish observation, interpretation, and uncertainty
8 Solve a varied problem using the measurement relation Independent problem solving Select the representation and justify it
9 Retrieve the repaired representation later Delayed review after sequence 1 Reconstruct the relation before checking
10 Complete a mixed selection problem Problem selection and transfer toward later work Explain why this representation fits
11–12 Leave uncommitted Recovery capacity If disruption occurs, apply the reduced route or one justified revision decision

Checks. Ten scheduled study blocks plus two reserve blocks equal the twelve-block boundary. Concept repair precedes both dependent problems. Preparation precedes the lab, and debrief follows it. The plan contains all five required functions. Retrieval appears before feedback and later than the relevant learning episode. No unsupervised practical route is introduced.

Interpretation. The plan is balanced because the functions and dependencies are visible, not because every category has equal time. It is sustainable in the bounded sense used here because it fits the declared capacity and can absorb a disruption without automatically borrowing from the next week.

Revision rule. At weekly review, Rowan protects incomplete prerequisite repair, fixed work, and due retrieval; moves or splits required flexible work when feasible; replaces a failed medium with an equivalent route; and removes redundant optional work. The evidence, not guilt about an unchecked box, controls the choice.

Self-explanation prompt. Which decision most strongly prevents sequence 8 from becoming blind trial and error, and what evidence would make you change that decision next week?

The decisive choice is to place the representation repair before the varied problem. If fresh unaided evidence shows that representation is now secure, later plans can reduce that scaffold and allocate capacity to a new error or transfer task.

Source Note
claims claim-plan-balance, claim-course-cycle, claim-retrieval-spacing, claim-error-directed-revision, claim-recovery-route, claim-four-pass-model, claim-faded-supportsources source-premed-graph, source-premed-syllabus, source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence

The example independently applies the course functions and recovery protocol. Retrieval/spacing and the move from modelled to independent work follow the bounded learning evidence; the particular learner, block count, errors, and schedule are fictional.

scenes.title: Reconstruct the passes before checking
scenes.contentHtml:
Reconstruct the passes before checking

Close or cover the earlier scene. Write before reading the feedback.

Check
id check-reconstruct-four-passes
  1. Reconstruct the four planning passes in order.
  2. For each pass, name the planning error it prevents.
  3. Explain why “review for two hours on Sunday” is insufficient evidence that retrieval and spacing are represented.

Feedback after attempting

  1. Bound capacity prevents an unlimited task list from masquerading as a feasible week.
  2. Place dependencies and fixed work prevents dependent problems or labs from being scheduled before their prerequisites and required preparation.
  3. Distribute learning functions prevents one vague activity from being counted as concept learning, independent problem solving, lab evidence, and delayed review at once.
  4. Reserve and revise prevents every disruption from becoming automatic carryover into future capacity.

The Sunday label is incomplete because it does not say what will be retrieved, whether an attempt occurs before feedback, or whether the contact is separated from the original learning. A defensible entry names the earlier target, the operation, the evidence, and the relation to the earlier episode.

Try a changed case: a plan has a Tuesday concept explanation and a Friday “review” entry that asks the learner to reconstruct and apply the concept before checking. Which pass makes the Friday contact visible, and which pass verifies that Tuesday occurs first?

Answer: distributing learning functions identifies the delayed retrieval job; dependency-aware placement checks the order. The capacity and revision passes still matter because both entries must fit and remain adaptable.

Schedule another retrieval of the four passes at the next weekly review before opening your template. Change the prompt by using a disrupted week rather than repeating these exact words.

Source Note
claims claim-retrieval-spacing, claim-four-pass-modelsources source-premed-graph, source-premed-syllabus, source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence

The prompt-before-feedback and delayed-contact design follows the repository's retrieval and spacing synthesis. The four-pass answer is the lesson's declared planning model, whose exact labels remain an instructional inference.

scenes.title: A full calendar is not a sustainable plan
scenes.contentHtml:
A full calendar is not a sustainable plan

Consider two plans for the same fictional twelve-block capacity.

  • Plan A assigns a task to all twelve blocks and says, “If anything slips, move it to next week.”
  • Plan B schedules ten blocks, protects two as recovery capacity, and states which tasks are protected, replaceable, or removable after evidence arrives.

Before reading on, predict which plan better preserves the required learning functions after one flexible block becomes unavailable. Explain the mechanism, not just the choice.

Misconception
id misconception-full-calendar

The tempting model is: “Unused space is waste, so the strongest plan fills every available block. Recovery can happen after all scheduled work is complete.”

This model cannot explain how Plan A absorbs the lost block without exceeding its capacity boundary or borrowing from a later week. Moving the task forward preserves the task name but not feasibility. If the same rule repeats, the next week begins with work that its own capacity never included.

The replacement model treats recovery capacity (term-recovery-capacity) as a constraint. Plan B can absorb one disruption inside the declared week. If no disruption occurs, the reserve can remain recovery, support the course's reduced route, or be allocated only through a deliberate revision decision. It is not silently counted as proof of additional study.

Compare the plans on the same event: the unavailable block contained optional enrichment, while a due retrieval and prerequisite repair remain scheduled. Plan A copies the enrichment forward automatically. Plan B removes or defers it because it does not outrank the required or evidence-linked work.

Recheck: if the unavailable block instead contains required lab preparation for a fixed supervised session, what changes? Plan B protects that preparation, then uses reserve or removes lower-priority work. “Protect recovery” does not mean “never use reserve”; it means the plan controls when and why reserve is used.

This is a boundary error used to discriminate two plan models. It is not a claim that every learner who fills a calendar holds the same belief or has the same circumstances.

Source Note
claims claim-recovery-route, claim-four-pass-modelsources source-premed-graph, source-premed-syllabus, source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence

The course contract directly supports protected recovery, a reduced route, and re-entry without backlog dumping. The capacity-and-reserve explanation is the lesson's bounded synthesis; it does not establish a health outcome or universal reserve percentage.

scenes.title: Fade the planning support
scenes.contentHtml:
Fade the planning support

Attempt each task before reading its feedback. The support decreases from a nearly complete ledger to an independent plan.

Task 1: complete the missing decisions

A fictional learner has eight usable blocks. A fixed supervised lab uses two. The draft already contains one concept block, one preparation block before the lab, the lab, and one debrief block after it. One cumulative retrieval contact is due. The error log names no current prerequisite gap.

Complete these decisions:

  1. Where should the due retrieval be placed, and what must its entry say?
  2. How many blocks remain after scheduling it?
  3. How will recovery capacity be represented?

Feedback

The retrieval must occur after its earlier learning episode, ask for an attempt before feedback, and name the earlier target and evidence. The stated draft uses five blocks: concept, preparation, two lab blocks, and debrief. One retrieval block makes six, leaving two blocks. Protecting both as reserve is defensible; scheduling one problem block and leaving one reserve is also defensible if the problem serves the current outcome and the eight-block boundary is not exceeded. The key is to distinguish scheduled study from genuine reserve.

Task 2: choose the stronger sequence

The error log now shows a repeated graph-translation error. Compare:

  • Sequence X: mixed problem set → graph-translation explanation → retry;
  • Sequence Y: contrasting graph representations → explanation of the governing relation → mixed problem → later retrieval.

Choose the stronger initial route and explain when you would fade its support.

Feedback

Sequence Y is stronger because it repairs the earliest evidenced gap before dependent mixed selection. Fade the contrasting-example scaffold after the learner independently selects and explains the graph relation in varied problems. Keep a later retrieval contact because immediate success is not the same as delayed retention.

Task 3: construct independently

Use this fictional evidence record:

  • eleven usable blocks;
  • one two-block fixed supervised practical;
  • one concept prerequisite and one preparation task required before it;
  • one debrief after it;
  • a MOD error in selecting a model for a dependent problem;
  • one delayed retrieval contact due;
  • one optional enrichment task; and
  • one block that may lose connectivity.

Construct a chronological text ledger. For every scheduled activity, name its learning function, dependency or timing reason, evidence, and contingency. Protect recovery capacity. At weekly review, give one protect, move, split, replace, or remove decision for the optional enrichment.

Self-check and next step

A defensible response:

  • uses no more than eleven blocks;
  • places concept and preparation before the supervised practical and debrief after it;
  • repairs the model-selection error before another dependent problem;
  • includes a later retrieval attempt before feedback;
  • represents concept, problem, lab, review, and recovery functions;
  • prepares an offline text route for the connectivity risk; and
  • removes or defers optional enrichment if required or error-linked work needs the capacity.

If one relation is missing, repair only that relation and retry with a new order. If several are missing, return to the four-pass ledger and annotate one pass at a time. If the task is already easy, hide the checklist and solve the transfer scene without it.

Source Note
claims claim-plan-balance, claim-course-cycle, claim-retrieval-spacing, claim-error-directed-revision, claim-recovery-route, claim-four-pass-model, claim-faded-supportsources source-premed-graph, source-premed-syllabus, source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence

The progression models the repository's complete-example-to-independent-practice route and the course's evidence-directed scheduling requirements. All scenarios and numbers are fictional; they do not prescribe personal workload.

scenes.title: Adapt the model to an access-constrained week
scenes.contentHtml:
Adapt the model to an access-constrained week

The surface problem changes. You must now preserve the planning relations when the preferred medium is unreliable.

Transfer task

A fictional learner has ten usable blocks, intermittent connectivity, and one fixed institutionally supervised practical. A cumulative review is due. The latest error-log entry shows accurate verbal recall but weak transfer to a graph. Midweek access may remove one online block.

Without using private personal details, create a text plan that:

  1. bounds capacity at ten blocks;
  2. places practical preparation before the fixed supervised session and debrief after it;
  3. targets the graph-transfer error before a dependent problem;
  4. includes concept learning, problems, lab preparation/session/debrief, delayed review, and recovery;
  5. provides an offline route for every learning operation that does not require institutional equipment; and
  6. states what will be protected, replaced, split, moved, or removed if the online block disappears.

Do not solve an experimental procedure or replace the supervised practical with home activity. The access adaptation concerns preparation, explanation, retrieval, graph interpretation, and response recording.

What must remain invariant

The calendar medium can change, but these relations cannot:

  • scheduled work fits the capacity boundary;
  • prerequisite repair precedes dependent use;
  • the fixed practical remains supervised;
  • retrieval occurs before feedback and after earlier learning;
  • each required learning function has evidence;
  • recovery capacity or a reduced route remains explicit; and
  • next-week changes follow completion and error evidence.

An equivalent offline route might download or print the bounded explanation, graph prompt, and response template during reliable access, then accept a typed or handwritten interpretation. “Look at the graph later” is not equivalent because it removes the current reasoning operation.

Evaluate your adaptation

Read your plan chronologically. For each entry, say aloud or write: “This occurs now because …; it will produce …; if unavailable, I will ….” If the sentence has no dependency, evidence, or contingency, revise the entry rather than adding decoration.

After the week, use the fresh graph response to decide whether to fade the contrasting examples, protect another repair, or schedule a changed delayed prompt. Do not infer mastery from completion alone.

Source Note
claims claim-plan-balance, claim-retrieval-spacing, claim-error-directed-revision, claim-recovery-route, claim-four-pass-modelsources source-premed-graph, source-premed-syllabus, source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence

The transfer task preserves the course's required functions, delayed retrieval, error-directed recovery, and accessibility-equivalent operation. The access case is fictional, and the exact contingency sequence is an instructional application rather than a universal rule.

scenes.title: Turn the plan into a weekly control loop
scenes.contentHtml:
Turn the plan into a weekly control loop

The four passes build one week, but the outcome requires more than a static calendar. The plan becomes sustainable in the bounded sense when it forms a control loop:

  1. Plan: bound capacity, place dependencies, distribute functions, and protect reserve.
  2. Perform: attempt the scheduled learning operations and record the evidence actually produced.
  3. Inspect: compare completion, correctness, explanation, transfer, confidence, and access with what the plan expected.
  4. Revise: protect, move, split, replace, or remove work from the next plan.

Weekly review from memory

Before opening the old plan, reconstruct:

  • the capacity boundary;
  • the five required functions;
  • the four passes;
  • the strongest current error-to-action chain; and
  • the rule that prevents automatic backlog.

Then inspect the record. Ask:

Question Decision it supports
Which required or prerequisite work still lacks evidence? Protect or move
Which task was too large but still coherent when divided? Split
Which medium failed while the learning operation remained required? Replace with an equivalent route
Which work became redundant, optional, or unrelated to the mapped outcome? Remove
Which earlier learning now needs a changed retrieval prompt? Schedule delayed review
Which constraint needs explicit reserve or a reduced route? Protect recovery capacity

The next plan is not a moral score for the previous week. It is a revised model based on evidence and constraints.

Delayed retrieval link

At the start of the next science module, build its first week without opening this template. Explain why each problem, lab, and retrieval contact occupies its position. Later lessons should reactivate this outcome whenever an error log changes the order or amount of prerequisite work.

If disruption leaves only the course's minimum viable route (term-minimum-viable-study-day), record that reduced evidence truthfully and begin the next review from it. Do not mark the abandoned full plan as complete, and do not dump its entire task list into the next week.

Source Note
claims claim-plan-balance, claim-retrieval-spacing, claim-error-directed-revision, claim-recovery-route, claim-four-pass-modelsources source-premed-graph, source-premed-syllabus, source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence

The control loop connects the graph outcome to the course's error, recovery, and delayed-retrieval rules. Its exact four-pass and five-decision form is an original planning representation, not a claim of universal optimality.

scenes.title: Study-plan mastery evidence
scenes.contentHtml:
Study-plan mastery evidence

Complete the items in assessment.json without opening their answer records until after each attempt. The assessment is untimed and retakeable.

The four tasks require you to:

  1. repair an overfilled plan without inventing capacity;
  2. build a complete week from constraints, dependencies, a fixed supervised lab, error evidence, a due retrieval contact, and an access boundary;
  3. choose evidence-based next-week revisions rather than carrying every task forward; and
  4. transfer the planning model to a week with intermittent connectivity.

Use a chronological text ledger, table, calendar, or equivalent structured response. Every route must expose the same decisions: capacity, fixed work, dependencies, learning functions, evidence, contingency, and revision. Do not provide personal health, disability, work, or caregiving details; use the fictional evidence supplied.

Mastery rule

The response record must show all of these relations across the open tasks:

  • the stated capacity boundary is respected;
  • concept learning, problems, supervised lab work or preparation/debrief, delayed review, and recovery are all represented;
  • prerequisites and preparation precede dependent work;
  • at least one later retrieval contact asks for an attempt before feedback;
  • recovery capacity or a defensible reduced route is explicit;
  • the named error changes a later action; and
  • incomplete work is protected, moved, split, replaced, or removed for an evidence-linked reason.

No single category can compensate for a missing dependency or an invented capacity block.

Feedback and remediation

After each attempt, compare the response with the item reasoning and analytic rubric:

  • If capacity is exceeded, rebuild only the boundary and remove or defer the lowest-priority flexible work.
  • If categories are present but order is wrong, annotate every prerequisite and fixed event, then resequence.
  • If review is only rereading or immediate exposure, add a later reconstruction before feedback and name its target.
  • If recovery is only a label on scheduled work, create genuinely uncommitted capacity or state the reduced route.
  • If all misses move forward, apply protect, move, split, replace, or remove to each one.
  • If access changes the assessed operation, build a text/offline equivalent that preserves the reasoning.

Retry with a new fictional constraint and error record. Do not replay the same answer sequence. If the evidence-to-action chain remains unclear, return to topic-orientation-metacognition-and-error-log; if retrieval and spacing remain unclear, return to topic-orientation-mastery-and-retrieval.

Passing this assessment supports the mapped planning outcome within the lesson's conditions. It does not prove a plan was followed, establish delayed mastery, guarantee performance, or authorize clinical or laboratory activity.

Source Note
claims claim-plan-balance, claim-retrieval-spacing, claim-error-directed-revision, claim-recovery-route, claim-four-pass-modelsources source-premed-graph, source-premed-syllabus, source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence

The assessment criteria operationalize the graph outcome and course recovery/retrieval rules. The four-pass rubric is an original instructional synthesis whose validity remains subject to independent review and learner evidence.

scenes.title: Evidence and rights trail
scenes.contentHtml:
Evidence and rights trail

Use references.json for the complete source registry, exact locators, access dates, rights bases, and agent-access records. Use claims.json to inspect which source supports each material claim and the scenes where that claim appears.

Use ATTRIBUTION.md for the CC BY 4.0 contribution notice, source-use summary, third-party-asset statement, and agent provenance. This scene is navigation only and introduces no additional teaching claim.

aiAssistance.disclosure: Codex (OpenAI GPT-5), Codex CLI 0.144.6, materially audited and revised this preserved local draft in isolated author run codex-author-prem-lpp-005-20260720-bc40b733-5b22-4848-b972-6d555a0ffa88 under accountable principal github:VSBDev. The recorded SHA-256 digest covers the exact discloseable author-task payload supplied to this run plus the canonical author-role procedure read at run start, excluding hidden provider instructions and separately versioned repository authorities. This is author provenance, not independent review or evidence of learner effectiveness.

items.prompt: A fictional learner has twelve usable blocks. The draft schedules twelve study tasks, including a fixed supervised lab, but leaves no recovery capacity. A lower-priority optional reading duplicates a concept explanation already scheduled. What is the best first repair?

options.text: Remove or defer the duplicate optional reading and protect that block as recovery capacity.
Cancel the fixed supervised lab so every other task remains.
Declare a thirteenth usable block without changing the capacity evidence.
Keep all tasks and relabel the final problem set as recovery.

answer.summary: Remove or defer the lowest-value duplicate before inventing capacity or deleting required work.
answer.reasoning: The capacity boundary is twelve blocks, so the repair must occur within twelve. The fixed lab is not flexible, and relabelling a required task does not create reserve. Because the optional reading duplicates an already scheduled learning function, removing or deferring it preserves the required balance and creates genuine recovery capacity. Next, verify that prerequisite concept work still precedes dependent problems.
acceptableResponses: Remove or defer the duplicate optional reading and protect reserve.
commonErrors: Inventing an extra block makes the plan contradict its capacity evidence.
Calling a problem set recovery changes the label but not the workload.
Deleting the fixed supervised lab ignores a stated constraint and removes a required learning function.

items.prompt: Construct a one-week plan for the fictional evidence record. State the capacity boundary, place all required learning functions, preserve prerequisite order, include a delayed retrieval contact, protect recovery capacity, and give one equivalent text/offline route.
items.stimulus: Fourteen self-defined blocks are realistically usable. Two blocks are occupied by a fixed supervised lab. Before the lab, the learner must acquire a bounded measurement concept and complete one preparation problem. The error log shows a representation-translation gap in the preceding topic. One later cumulative retrieval contact is due. Connectivity is reliable on only three study days.

answer.correct: A defensible plan uses no more than fourteen blocks, protects explicit reserve, places concept and preparation before the supervised lab, includes problem solving and post-lab debrief, schedules the due delayed retrieval, targets the representation-translation error, and supplies an equivalent offline text route.
answer.summary: Make constraints, functions, order, evidence, and contingency visible.
answer.reasoning: A complete response begins with the observed fourteen-block boundary rather than a wish list. The fixed two-block lab anchors the week. Prerequisite concept work and the preparation problem precede it; debrief follows it. The error record justifies a contrasting representation task, and the due cumulative contact is scheduled later than its original learning. At least one block remains protected or a clear reduced route is declared. Because connectivity is limited, readings, prompts, and response templates needed offline are prepared during a reliable-access block.
acceptableResponses: Any sequence that satisfies the stated capacity, dependencies, five outcome functions, evidence-directed repair, delayed retrieval, reserve, and equivalent offline route.
commonErrors: Using all fourteen blocks for tasks leaves no recovery capacity.
Placing the preparation problem or concept acquisition after the lab reverses the dependency.
Lab attendance alone omits preparation or debrief evidence.
Immediate rereading is not the due delayed retrieval contact.
An online-only plan ignores the stated access constraint.

items.prompt: At weekly review, an optional enrichment reading is incomplete, the required concept check is secure, and two problem attempts reveal the same prerequisite representation error. Select the two strongest revision decisions for next week.

options.text: Protect a targeted contrasting representation task before the next dependent problem set.
Remove or defer the optional enrichment unless it serves a current outcome.
Copy every incomplete and completed task into next week.
Replace all problem practice with another full reading of the secure concept.

answer.summary: Target the observed prerequisite error and prevent optional work from becoming automatic backlog.
answer.reasoning: The repeated representation error identifies the earliest insecure dependency, so a contrasting representation task should be protected before more dependent problems. The optional enrichment can be removed or deferred because it does not outrank the mapped repair. Copying every task creates an evidence-blind backlog, while rereading the secure concept does not address the observed error.
acceptableResponses: Targeted representation repair plus removal or deferral of optional enrichment.
commonErrors: Treating every incomplete task as compulsory ignores outcome priority and current evidence.
Repeating secure content uses capacity without repairing the stated prerequisite gap.

items.prompt: Adapt the four-pass model to the fictional access-constrained week, then explain which planning relations remain invariant and what will be revised after evidence arrives. Use generic labels rather than personal information.
items.stimulus: The learner has ten usable blocks, intermittent connectivity, one fixed institutionally supervised practical, and a new error-log entry showing accurate recall but weak transfer to a graph. A cumulative review is due. Midweek access may remove one online block.

answer.correct: A complete response bounds ten blocks, anchors the supervised practical, prepares offline materials during access, places graph-representation repair before dependent use, includes concept/problem/lab/review functions, protects reserve or a reduced route, and states an evidence-based next-week revision rule.
answer.summary: Adapt the medium and placement while preserving capacity, dependency, learning-function, retrieval, and revision relations.
answer.reasoning: Connectivity is a constraint, not a reason to remove the learning operation. The learner can download or print the necessary concept explanation, graph prompt, and response template during reliable access, then complete retrieval and explanation offline. The fixed practical remains institutionally supervised. The graph-transfer gap justifies a contrasting graph task before dependent problem use. Recovery capacity absorbs the possible missing online block; if the disruption occurs, the plan protects the prerequisite repair and due retrieval, then removes, replaces, splits, or moves lower-priority work rather than copying everything forward.
acceptableResponses: Any access-equivalent plan that preserves the same functions and decision relations within ten blocks and gives an evidence-linked revision rule.
commonErrors: Requiring constant connectivity changes access rather than adapting the plan.
Scheduling an unsupervised practical invents a safety route not authorized by the lesson.
Removing recovery capacity because the week is constrained makes the plan less able to absorb the named disruption.
Naming categories without ordering prerequisites or delayed review does not demonstrate the planning model.

criteria.description: Selects a repair that respects the observed capacity, preserves fixed required work, and creates genuine recovery capacity.
States and respects the fourteen-block boundary while protecting explicit recovery capacity or a defensible reduced route.
Represents concept learning, problems, the supervised lab with preparation or debrief, delayed review, and recovery.
Places prerequisite concept and preparation before dependent work and puts the retrieval contact after the earlier learning episode.
Targets the representation error and supplies an equivalent offline text route for limited-connectivity periods.
Protects targeted repair of the earliest evidenced prerequisite error before more dependent work.
Removes or defers optional work rather than carrying every task forward automatically.
Uses no more than ten blocks, anchors the fixed supervised practical, and preserves reserve or a reduced route.
Preserves dependency order and the distinct concept, problem, lab, delayed-review, and recovery functions.
Adapts materials and responses to an equivalent offline route without requiring personal disclosure or unsupervised practical work.
Uses the graph-transfer error to select targeted repair and states a protect, move, split, replace, or remove revision rule for new evidence.

sources.title: Evidence synthesis: writing university-grade learning content
sources.version: Rapid evidence synthesis at repository commit 0cd91ddd6189b93cadc51e21d37b006854ab3104

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Only bounded synthesis conclusions and limitations were used; the lesson procedure, cases, representations, prompts, and feedback were created independently.

agentAccess.notes: This is repository-owned research prose covered by the project content license and supplied in the authorized checkout. No access control or agent-ingestion restriction was found.

claims.statement: The assigned Premed outcome requires a sustainable weekly plan that balances concept learning, problems, labs, review, and recovery.
claims.scope: The PREM-01.05 curriculum outcome and the course study-cycle design, not a universal prescription for all programs.

claims.statement: The EmbeddedKnowledge course separates orientation and acquisition from retrieval, problem solving, investigation, application, reflection, and planning, so a weekly plan must make different learning functions visible.
claims.scope: The adopted EmbeddedKnowledge Premed course cycle; learners may combine functions in one block when they can still name the evidence each function produces.
claims.uncertainty: The syllabus does not establish one optimal proportion of weekly time for each function.

claims.statement: Retrieval before answer reveal and later contacts distributed across time generally support more durable learning than repeated exposure alone, while useful spacing depends on the retention goal and task conditions.
claims.scope: Learning tasks and populations represented in the cited synthesis, applied here as a bounded planning default rather than a fixed interval formula.
claims.uncertainty: Optimal gaps vary with prior knowledge, material, retrieval success, feedback, and desired retention interval; this lesson prescribes no universal spacing schedule.

claims.statement: The course recovery protocol begins by naming the failing node and error class, checking the earliest insecure prerequisite, and selecting targeted varied practice before a later retry.
claims.scope: The EmbeddedKnowledge Premed mastery-recovery protocol; it does not diagnose why a learner had limited time or access.

claims.statement: The course design protects recovery periods, permits a reduced minimum viable route during disruption, and calls for compassionate re-entry rather than an automatic backlog dump.
claims.scope: The adopted course route and motivation-protection rules, not a medical claim about rest or a guarantee against overload.
claims.uncertainty: Individual capacity and support needs vary and may require human or institutional accommodation outside this lesson.

claims.statement: A capacity boundary, dependency-aware placement, distributed learning functions, and evidence-driven reserve-and-revise rule form a defensible planning model for this outcome.
claims.scope: An original instructional synthesis for PREM-01.05; the four passes are a transparent reasoning aid, not a validated universal productivity system.
claims.uncertainty: No retained source compares this exact four-pass procedure with alternative personal planning systems.

claims.statement: Initial worked reasoning followed by completion, supported, and independent problems is a defensible novice progression, with assistance reduced as the learner can perform the planning decisions.
claims.scope: Novice instruction and the planning tasks in this lesson; support should remain available when prerequisite knowledge or access requires it.
claims.uncertainty: The useful rate of fading depends on prior knowledge and observed performance.

terms.definition: A learner-declared limit on usable study opportunities after fixed commitments and access constraints are represented, before study tasks are allocated.
A learner-defined unit of usable study opportunity. Its duration may vary, so blocks are planning units rather than universal measures of effort.
The instructional job of a planned activity, such as concept construction, retrieval, problem solving, lab preparation or debrief, or evidence-based revision.
A later attempt to reconstruct or use earlier learning before answer reveal, separated from the initial learning episode by a planned interval.
Study-plan capacity deliberately left uncommitted or assigned to a reduced route so disruption can be absorbed without automatically borrowing from later weeks.
A time- or access-bound event that the weekly plan must represent before flexible study is placed, such as an institutionally supervised lab or an unavoidable non-study commitment.
A preselected alternative action used when the planned route is unavailable, while preserving the intended learning function when possible.
One of five explicit actions for an incomplete or ineffective planned task: protect, move, split, replace, or remove, justified by outcome and error evidence.
The course's reduced re-entry route during disruption: a small retrieval action, one worked example, and one explicit next action, used without claiming that the full weekly plan was completed.

attributionHtml:
Attribution and rights

Lesson contribution

PREM-LPP-005, version 0.1.0, is original educational content prepared for EmbeddedKnowledge under the accountable principal github:VSBDev.

Except where stated otherwise, the lesson metadata, prose, fictional examples, tables, prompts, answer logic, rubric language, glossary, and authoring brief are offered under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license (CC BY 4.0).

Suggested attribution: Based on EmbeddedKnowledge, “PREM-LPP-005 — Personal study plan: capacity, learning functions, and recovery,” version 0.1.0, by VSBDev, licensed CC BY 4.0. Changes: describe changes.

Source use

The four records in references.json were used only for bounded factual reference and original synthesis:

  • source-premed-graph supports the exact target, prerequisite, and graph position.
  • source-premed-syllabus supports the course learning cycle, recovery protocol, reduced route, and scheduling boundaries.
  • source-content-standard supports the instructional requirements applied to retrieval, practice, feedback, transfer, accessibility, and recovery.
  • source-authoring-evidence supports bounded learning-science conclusions and their stated limitations.

No third-party wording, question, table, figure, analogy, dataset extract, image, audio, video, or other expressive material is copied, adapted, or redistributed. Source organization was not used as the lesson organization. There are no third-party assets.

Agent disclosure

Material authoring assistance was provided by Codex / OpenAI / GPT-5 / Codex CLI 0.144.6 in isolated author run codex-author-prem-lpp-005-20260720-bc40b733-5b22-4848-b972-6d555a0ffa88 under accountable principal github:VSBDev.

The material-instructions digest is sha256:eb849c052fda3e38075e23774c0add6a1ffe20d818dee7a2ee935389c6547048. It covers the UTF-8 concatenation of the exact discloseable author-task payload supplied to this run, a newline delimiter, and the canonical author-role procedure read at run start. It does not claim to cover hidden provider instructions or the separately versioned repository authorities consulted during the run.

This disclosure records authorship provenance only. It is not an independent academic, learning-design, or accessibility-and-rights review; it does not establish learner effectiveness, approval, adjudication, publication, or course coverage.
